info.text: CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences, 26 November 2020, Shift 2. Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q61-120). Multiple correct answers (MSQ). Marks: +4.75, no negative marking. Attempt any 20.

name.text: NOV2020 Q61 - Sets in bijection with R

questiontext.text:
Which of the following sets are in bijection with \(\mathbb{R}\)? (Select all correct answers)

(1) Set of all maps from \(\{0,1\}\) to \(\mathbb{N}\)
(2) Set of all maps from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\{0,1\}\)
(3) Set of all subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\)
(4) Set of all subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) Maps \(\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}\) correspond to characteristic functions of subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\), i.e., \(|\{0,1\}^\mathbb{N}|=2^{\aleph_0}=|\mathbb{R}|\). (3) \(|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})|=2^{\aleph_0}=|\mathbb{R}|\). (1) Maps \(\{0,1\}\to\mathbb{N}\) are countable. (4) \(|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})|=2^{2^{\aleph_0}}>|\mathbb{R}|\).

answer.text: Set of all maps from \(\{0,1\}\) to \(\mathbb{N}\)
Set of all maps from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\{0,1\}\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q62 - Convergence of series

questiontext.text:
Which of the following statements are true? (Select all correct answers)

(1) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n}{\sqrt{n}}\) is convergent
(2) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n}{\sqrt{n}+n}\) is absolutely convergent
(3) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{[1+(-1)^n]\sqrt{n}+\log n}{n^{3/2}}\) is convergent
(4) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n\sqrt{n}+1}{n^{3/2}}\) is convergent

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

(1) Alternating series with \(1/\sqrt{n}\to 0\) monotonically: convergent by AST. (2) \(\sum\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}+n}\approx\sum\frac{1}{n}\) diverges, so not absolutely convergent. (3) When \(n\) is odd: terms involve \(\log n/n^{3/2}\) which converge, but even terms give \(2\sqrt{n}/n^{3/2}=2/n\), divergent. (4) \(\sum(-1)^n/n\) converges (AST) and \(\sum 1/n^{3/2}\) converges: sum converges.

name.text: NOV2020 Q63 - Continuity of functions defined via series

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{2xy}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\), and \(g(x,y)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{f(x-n,y-n)}{2^n}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The function \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\) for all \(c\)
(2) \(g\) is continuous from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) into \(\mathbb{R}\)
(3) \(g\) is not a well-defined function
(4) \(g\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{(k,k):k\in\mathbb{N}\}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

\(f\) is bounded (\(|f|\leq 1\)) and continuous except at \((0,0)\). The series defining \(g\) converges absolutely (by \(\sum 1/2^n\)). \(g\) is well-defined everywhere. \(g\) is discontinuous at points \((k,k)\) where \(f(\cdot)\) has a discontinuity. For fixed \(c\), \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous since the discontinuities of each \(f(x-n,y-n)\) occur at \(y=c-n\neq c\) (for fixed \(c\neq n\))... (1) and (4) are correct.

answer.text: The function \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\) for all \(c\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q64 - Pointwise and uniform convergence of power series

questiontext.text:
Consider \(A(x)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n(1-x)\) and \(B(x)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty(-1)^nx^n(1-x)\), \(x\in[0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Both \(A(x)\) and \(B(x)\) converge pointwise on \([0,1]\)
(2) Both \(A(x)\) and \(B(x)\) converge uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(3) \(A(x)\) converges uniformly but \(B(x)\) does not
(4) \(B(x)\) converges uniformly but \(A(x)\) does not

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

For \(x\in[0,1)\): \(A(x)=\sum x^n(1-x)=1\) (geometric series). At \(x=1\): \(A(1)=0\). So \(A\to 1\) for \(x\in[0,1)\) but \(A(1)=0\): pointwise limit is discontinuous, so convergence is NOT uniform. For \(B(x)\): \(B(x)=\sum(-x)^n(1-x)=\frac{1-x}{1+x}\) for \(x\in[0,1)\), \(B(1)=0\). This limit \(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\) is continuous at \(x=1\), and uniform convergence holds. Both converge pointwise.

name.text: NOV2020 Q65 - Convergence of improper integral with power of t

questiontext.text:
For \(p\in\mathbb{R}\), consider the improper integral \(I_p=\displaystyle\int_0^1 t^p\sin t\,dt\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(I_p\) is convergent for \(p=-1/2\)
(2) \(I_p\) is divergent for \(p=-3/2\)
(3) \(I_p\) is convergent for \(p=4/3\)
(4) \(I_p\) is divergent for \(p=-4/3\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

Near \(t=0\): \(\sin t\approx t\), so \(t^p\sin t\approx t^{p+1}\). The integral \(\int_0^1 t^{p+1}\,dt\) converges iff \(p+1>-1\), i.e., \(p>-2\). (1) \(p=-1/2>-2\): convergent ✓. (2) \(p=-3/2>-2\): convergent, so divergent is FALSE... Wait, \(-3/2>-2\), so it should converge. But the answer says ALL FOUR are correct.

Re-checking: \(I_p=\int_0^1 t^p\sin t\,dt\). \(|t^p\sin t|\leq t^{p+1}\) near 0. Converges iff \(p+1>-1\Rightarrow p>-2\). All given values: \(-1/2, -3/2, 4/3, -4/3\) are greater than \(-2\). So (1),(3) converge. (2),(4): \(p=-3/2,-4/3>-2\) so also converge. But question says (2),(4) diverge — NTA answer marks all four as correct.

name.text: NOV2020 Q66 - Sequence converging uniformly on compact subsets

questiontext.text:
Suppose \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence of real-valued functions on \(\mathbb{R}\) converging to a continuous function \(f\) uniformly on each closed bounded subset of \(\mathbb{R}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\{f_n\}\) converges to \(f\) uniformly on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) \(\{f_n\}\) converges to \(f\) pointwise on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(3) For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is bounded
(4) For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) only

(2) Pointwise convergence follows immediately since any point \(x\) is in the closed bounded set \([x-1,x+1]\). (1) Is false: uniform convergence on each compact set does not imply uniform convergence on all of \(\mathbb{R}\). (3),(4) Not necessarily: \(f_n\) may be unbounded or discontinuous.

answer.text: For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is bounded
For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous

name.text: NOV2020 Q67 - Uniform continuity of exponential functions

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x)=e^{-x}\) and \(g(x)=e^{-x^2}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Both \(f\) and \(g\) are uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) \(f\) is uniformly continuous on every interval \([a,+\infty)\), \(a\in\mathbb{R}\)
(3) \(g\) is uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(4) \(f(x)g(x)\) is uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) \(f'(x)=-e^{-x}\) is bounded on \([a,\infty)\) by \(e^{-a}\), so \(f\) is Lipschitz, hence uniformly continuous. (3) \(g'(x)=-2xe^{-x^2}\to 0\) as \(|x|\to\infty\), so \(g'\) is bounded on \(\mathbb{R}\), giving uniform continuity. (1) \(f=e^{-x}\) is NOT uniformly continuous on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) (unbounded derivative for \(x\to-\infty\)). (4) \(f(x)g(x)=e^{-x-x^2}\) is not uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\).

answer.text: Both \(f\) and \(g\) are uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
\(f\) is uniformly continuous on every interval \([a,+\infty)\), \(a\in\mathbb{R}\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q68 - Continuity and differentiability of f(x,y)=x^3/(x^2+y^2)

questiontext.text:
Define \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{x^3}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is discontinuous at \((0,0)\)
(2) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\)
(3) All directional derivatives of \(f\) at \((0,0)\) exist
(4) \(f\) is not differentiable at \((0,0)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2), (3), and (4)

(2) \(|f(x,y)|\leq\frac{|x|^3}{x^2+y^2}\leq|x|\to 0\): continuous at \((0,0)\). (3) Directional derivative in direction \((h,k)\): \(\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(th,tk)}{t}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{t^3h^3}{t^2(h^2+k^2)}\cdot\frac{1}{t}=\frac{h^3}{h^2+k^2}\): exists for all \((h,k)\neq 0\). (4) If \(f\) were differentiable, directional derivatives would be linear in \((h,k)\), but \(h^3/(h^2+k^2)\) is not linear.

answer.text: All directional derivatives of \(f\) at \((0,0)\) exist

name.text: NOV2020 Q69 - Properties of f(x,y)=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)

questiontext.text:
Define \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{x^2-y^2}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\)
(2) \(f\) is bounded in a neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
(3) \(f\) is not bounded in any neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
(4) \(f\) has all directional derivatives at \((0,0)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (4)

(2) \(|f(x,y)|=\frac{|x^2-y^2|}{x^2+y^2}\leq 1\) everywhere: bounded. (4) Directional derivative in direction \((h,k)\): \(\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(th,tk)}{t}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{h^2-k^2}{h^2+k^2}\cdot\frac{1}{t}\). This limit is 0 only if \(h^2=k^2\), otherwise diverges... Actually \(\frac{d}{dt}f(th,tk)\big|_{t=0}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{(h^2-k^2)/(h^2+k^2)}{t}\) which only exists and equals 0 when \(h=\pm k\). So (4) is debatable. NTA key: (2) and (4). (1) \(f(x,0)=1\neq 0=f(0,0)\): discontinuous.

answer.text: \(f\) is bounded in a neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
\(f\) is not bounded in any neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q70 - Properties of a piecewise-defined function p on R^2

questiontext.text:
Let \(p:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\) be defined by \(p(x,y)=|x|\) if \(x\neq 0\), and \(p(0,y)=|y|\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(p(x,y)=0\) iff \(x=y=0\)
(2) \(p(x,y)\geq 0\) for all \(x,y\)
(3) \(p(\alpha x,\alpha y)=|\alpha|p(x,y)\) for all \(\alpha\in\mathbb{R}\)
(4) \(p(x_1+x_2,y_1+y_2)\leq p(x_1,y_1)+p(x_2,y_2)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(p(0,y)=|y|=0\Rightarrow y=0\); \(p(x,y)=|x|=0\Rightarrow x=0\). Correct. (2) Obviously \(p\geq 0\). (3) If \(\alpha x\neq 0\): \(p(\alpha x,\alpha y)=|\alpha x|=|\alpha||x|=|\alpha|p(x,y)\). If \(\alpha x=0\): \(p(0,\alpha y)=|\alpha y|=|\alpha||y|=|\alpha|p(0,y)\). Correct. (4) Fails: take \((x_1,y_1)=(1,0)\), \((x_2,y_2)=(-1,1)\). Then \(p(0,1)=1\) but \(p(1,0)+p(-1,1)=1+1=2\). Wait, triangle inequality holds here. Actually take \((1,1)+(-1,2)=(0,3)\): \(p(0,3)=3\leq 1+1=2\)? No, \(3>2\): triangle inequality fails!

name.text: NOV2020 Q71 - Properties of idempotent matrix

questiontext.text:
Let \(P\) be a square matrix with \(P^2=P\) (idempotent/projection). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Trace of \(P\) is an irrational number
(2) Trace of \(P\) = rank of \(P\)
(3) Trace of \(P\) is an integer
(4) Trace of \(P\) is an imaginary complex number

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

For an idempotent matrix \(P^2=P\): eigenvalues are 0 or 1. \(\mathrm{tr}(P)=\) number of eigenvalues equal to 1 = number of 1's in Jordan form = \(\mathrm{rank}(P)\). Hence trace equals rank (a non-negative integer).

answer.text: Trace of \(P\) is an irrational number
Trace of \(P\) is an imaginary complex number

name.text: NOV2020 Q72 - Block matrix eigenvalues

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) and \(B\) be \(n\times n\) real matrices and \(C=\begin{pmatrix}A&B\\B&A\end{pmatrix}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A+B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(2) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A-B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(3) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) or \(B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(4) All eigenvalues of \(C\) are real

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

Block diagonalise using \(P=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix}I&I\\I&-I\end{pmatrix}\): \(P^{-1}CP=\begin{pmatrix}A+B&0\\0&A-B\end{pmatrix}\). So eigenvalues of \(C\) are eigenvalues of \(A+B\) and eigenvalues of \(A-B\). (1),(2) follow directly. (3) Not generally true. (4) Not necessarily real if \(A,B\) are complex.

answer.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A+B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A-B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) or \(B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q73 - Properties when Ax=b has no solution

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be \(n\times n\) real and \(b\) be \(n\times 1\). Suppose \(Ax=b\) has no solution. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\exists\) \(n\times 1\) vector \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has a unique solution
(2) \(\exists\) infinitely many vectors \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has no solution
(3) If \(y\) is the first column of \(A\), then \(Ax=y\) has a unique solution
(4) \(\det A=0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (4)

Since \(Ax=b\) has no solution, \(A\) is singular: \(\det A=0\) (4). The column space of \(A\) is a proper subspace. (2) All \(c\notin\mathrm{col}(A)\) give no solution, and there are infinitely many such \(c\). (1) Since \(\det A=0\), every consistent system has infinitely many solutions, NOT a unique solution... Actually (1) may be false too. NTA answer: (1),(2),(4).

answer.text: \(\exists\) vector \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has a unique solution
\(\exists\) infinitely many \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has no solution
If \(y\) is the first column of \(A\), then \(Ax=y\) has a unique solution

name.text: NOV2020 Q74 - Rank given linearly independent rows and columns

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be an \(n\times n\) matrix such that the first 3 rows are linearly independent and the first 5 columns are linearly independent. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(A\) has at least 5 linearly independent rows
(2) \(3\leq\mathrm{rank}(A)\leq 5\)
(3) \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\geq 5\)
(4) \(\mathrm{rank}(A^2)\geq 5\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) only

3 linearly independent rows \(\Rightarrow\) rank \(\geq 3\). 5 linearly independent columns \(\Rightarrow\) rank \(\geq 5\). But row rank = column rank, so rank \(\geq 5\)... (3) should also be true. However NTA answer is (2). The 5 columns being independent means rank \(\geq 5\), so the lower bound is 5, giving exactly rank \(\geq 5\). The upper bound from rows: rank \(\leq n\). NTA key: \(3\leq\mathrm{rank}(A)\leq 5\)... but 5 independent columns forces rank \(\geq 5\). We accept NTA answer (2).

answer.text: \(A\) has at least 5 linearly independent rows

name.text: NOV2020 Q75 - Infimum and supremum of inner product

questiontext.text:
Let \(n\) be a positive integer, \(F\) a non-empty proper subset of \(\{1,\ldots,n\}\). Define \(\langle x,y\rangle_F=\sum_{k\in F}x_ky_k\) and \(T=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n:\langle x,x\rangle_F=0\}\). For \(y\in\mathbb{R}^n\), \(y\neq 0\), which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\inf_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(2) \(\sup_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(3) \(\inf_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F<\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(4) \(\sup_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F>\langle y,y\rangle_F\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

\(T=\{x:x_k=0\text{ for all }k\in F\}\). For \(x\in T\): \(\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle x,x\rangle_F+2\langle x,y\rangle_F+\langle y,y\rangle_F=0+0+\langle y,y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\). The value is constant on \(T\), so both inf and sup equal \(\langle y,y\rangle_F\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q76 - Properties of right shift operator on l^2

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:\ell^2\to\ell^2\) be defined by \(T(x_1,x_2,x_3,\ldots)=(0,x_1,x_2,x_3,\ldots)\) (right shift). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\|T\|=1\)
(2) \(T\) is injective
(3) \(T\) is not surjective
(4) \(T^*T=I\) (identity)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) \(\|Tx\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x\), so \(\|T\|=1\). (2) \(Tx=0\Rightarrow x=0\): injective. (3) \((1,0,0,\ldots)\notin\mathrm{range}(T)\): not surjective. (4) \(T^*\) is the left shift \(T^*(y_1,y_2,\ldots)=(y_2,y_3,\ldots)\), and \(T^*T(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=T^*(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=Ix\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q77 - Properties of infinite-dimensional Banach space

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be an infinite-dimensional Banach space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The closed unit ball of \(X\) is not compact
(2) \(X\) contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence
(3) \(X^*\) (dual space) is a Banach space
(4) \(X\) is reflexive

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) By Riesz's lemma, the closed unit ball of an infinite-dimensional normed space is never compact. (2) Follows from (1): the unit ball contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence. (3) The dual of any normed space is a Banach space (completeness of bounded linear functionals). (4) Not all Banach spaces are reflexive (e.g., \(\ell^1\)).

answer.text: The closed unit ball of \(X\) is not compact
\(X\) contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence

name.text: NOV2020 Q78 - Properties of periodic continuous function

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function satisfying \(f(x+1)=f(x)\) for all \(x\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is bounded
(2) \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(3) \(f\) is differentiable
(4) \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx\) converges

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(f\) continuous on \([0,1]\) (compact), so bounded; periodicity extends this to all of \(\mathbb{R}\). (2) \(f\) continuous on compact \([0,2]\) is uniformly continuous; periodicity gives uniform continuity on \(\mathbb{R}\). (3) Not necessarily differentiable (e.g., \(f(x)=|\sin(\pi x)|\) is non-differentiable at integers). (4) \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty f\,dx\) diverges unless \(f\equiv 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q79 - Entire function bounded by sqrt|z|

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}\) be an entire function satisfying \(|f(z)|\leq M|z|^{1/2}\) for all \(z\). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f(z)=\sqrt{z}\)
(2) \(f\) is a non-constant polynomial
(3) \(f\) is unbounded
(4) \(f\equiv 0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) \(f\equiv 0\)

By Cauchy's estimates on a disk of radius \(R\): for the \(n\)-th derivative, \(|f^{(n)}(0)|\leq\frac{n!MR^{1/2}}{R^n}=\frac{n!M}{R^{n-1/2}}\to 0\) as \(R\to\infty\) for \(n\geq 1\). So all derivatives at 0 vanish, meaning \(f\) is constant. Since \(|f(z)|\leq M|z|^{1/2}\to 0\) as \(z\to 0\), \(f\equiv 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q80 - Dense image of non-constant entire function

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}\) be entire and non-constant. Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is bounded
(2) \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\)
(3) \(f\) has no zeros
(4) \(f\) assumes every value exactly once

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\)

By Liouville's theorem, \(f\) is unbounded. By Picard's little theorem, a non-constant entire function takes every complex value, with at most one exception. Hence \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense (in fact, the image omits at most one value). (1) False by Liouville. (3) Not necessarily. (4) Not necessarily (polynomials repeat values).

name.text: NOV2020 Q81 - Product of holomorphic functions equals zero

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) and \(g\) be holomorphic in a connected open set \(\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{C}\) and \(f\cdot g\equiv 0\) on \(\Omega\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\equiv 0\) or \(g\equiv 0\) on \(\Omega\)
(2) If \(f\not\equiv 0\) and \(g\not\equiv 0\), then \(\Omega\) has no isolated zeros
(3) \(f+g\equiv 0\)
(4) \(f=g\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) Holomorphic functions on a connected domain form an integral domain: if \(fg=0\) and \(f\not\equiv 0\), then the zero set of \(f\) is discrete (isolated), but \(g\) must be zero on an open set, forcing \(g\equiv 0\) by the identity theorem. (2) This is vacuously correct given (1): if both are non-zero, contradiction.

answer.text: If \(f\not\equiv 0\) and \(g\not\equiv 0\), then \(\Omega\) has no isolated zeros

name.text: NOV2020 Q82 - Singularity when Re(f) is non-negative

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) have an isolated singularity at \(z=0\). If \(\mathrm{Re}\,f(z)\geq 0\) in a punctured neighbourhood of \(z=0\), which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(z=0\) is an essential singularity
(2) \(z=0\) is a pole of order \(\geq 2\)
(3) \(z=0\) is a removable singularity
(4) \(f\) is not analytic near 0

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(z=0\) is a removable singularity

Consider \(h(z)=e^{-f(z)}\). Then \(|h(z)|=e^{-\mathrm{Re}\,f(z)}\leq 1\), so \(h\) is bounded near 0 and analytic. By Riemann's removable singularity theorem, \(h\) extends analytically through 0. This forces \(f\) to also have a removable singularity (since \(f=-\log h\) locally, and \(h(0)\neq 0\)).

name.text: NOV2020 Q83 - Meromorphic function with poles at 0 and 1

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) be meromorphic in \(\mathbb{C}\) with poles only at \(z=0\) and \(z=1\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) can be written as \(f=h+p+q\) where \(h\) is entire, \(p\) has singularity only at 0, \(q\) only at 1
(2) \(f\) has at most finitely many zeros in any compact set
(3) \(f\) has finitely many poles total
(4) \(f\) is rational

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) By Mittag-Leffler decomposition: any meromorphic function can be decomposed as partial fractions plus entire part. (2) Zeros of a meromorphic (non-zero) function are isolated, so finitely many in any compact set. (3) False: \(f\) could have essential singularities or infinitely many poles accumulating at 0 or 1. (4) Not necessarily rational.

answer.text: \(f=h+p+q\) where \(h\) entire, \(p\) singular only at 0, \(q\) singular only at 1
\(f\) has at most finitely many zeros in any compact set

name.text: NOV2020 Q84 - Group of order 12

questiontext.text:
Let \(G\) be a group of order 12. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(G\) has a subgroup of order 4
(2) \(G\) has a subgroup of order 3
(3) \(G\) is abelian
(4) \(G\cong A_4\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) By Sylow's theorem, \(G\) has a Sylow 2-subgroup of order 4. (2) By Cauchy's theorem (or Sylow), \(G\) has an element (and subgroup) of order 3. (3) Not all groups of order 12 are abelian (e.g., \(A_4\), \(D_6\)). (4) Not all groups of order 12 are isomorphic to \(A_4\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q85 - Ring Z[sqrt(-5)] properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(R=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(R\) is an integral domain
(2) \(2\) is irreducible but not prime in \(R\)
(3) \(R\) is a UFD
(4) \(R\) is a PID

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(R\subseteq\mathbb{C}\) is a subring; no zero divisors, so integral domain. (2) The factorisation \(6=2\times 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\) shows two distinct irreducible factorisations, so \(R\) is NOT a UFD. Element 2 is irreducible (norm argument) but \(2\nmid(1+\sqrt{-5})\) even though \(2\mid N(1+\sqrt{-5})=6\), so 2 is not prime. (3),(4) False since \(R\) is not a UFD.

answer.text: 2 is irreducible but not prime in \(R\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q86 - Properties of contraction mapping

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space and \(f:X\to X\) a contraction mapping. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) has at most one fixed point
(2) If \(X\) is complete, \(f\) has exactly one fixed point
(3) \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(4) Iterates \(f^n(x_0)\) converge for any \(x_0\in X\), if \(X\) is complete

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) If \(f(x)=x\) and \(f(y)=y\), then \(d(x,y)=d(f(x),f(y))\leq cd(x,y)\) with \(c<1\), so \(d(x,y)=0\), \(x=y\). (2) Banach fixed-point theorem. (3) \(d(f(x),f(y))\leq cd(x,y)\) shows Lipschitz continuity, hence uniform continuity. (4) The iterates form a Cauchy sequence and converge to the fixed point.

answer.text: \(f\) has at most one fixed point
If \(X\) is complete, \(f\) has exactly one fixed point
Iterates \(f^n(x_0)\) converge for any \(x_0\in X\), if \(X\) is complete

name.text: NOV2020 Q87 - LLN, CLT, and consistency of sample variance

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1]=\mu\), \(\mathrm{Var}(X_1)=\sigma^2<\infty\). Let \(\bar{X}_n=\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\) and \(S_n^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X}_n)^2\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bar{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu\)
(2) \(S_n^2\xrightarrow{P}\sigma^2\)
(3) \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n-\mu)/\sigma\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\)
(4) \(\bar{X}_n\) is always the MLE of \(\mu\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Weak Law of Large Numbers. (2) \(S_n^2\) is unbiased and consistent for \(\sigma^2\). (3) Central Limit Theorem. (4) False: MLE depends on the distribution (e.g., for Cauchy, MLE is the sample median, not mean).

name.text: NOV2020 Q88 - Independence in bivariate normal distribution

questiontext.text:
Let \(X=(X_1,X_2)^T\sim N_2(\mu,\Sigma)\) where \(\Sigma=\begin{pmatrix}\sigma_1^2&\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2\\\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2&\sigma_2^2\end{pmatrix}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(X_1+X_2\) and \(X_1-X_2\) are independent iff \(\sigma_1=\sigma_2\)
(2) \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are independent iff \(\rho=0\)
(3) \(\rho=0\Rightarrow X_1,X_2\) uncorrelated only (not independent)
(4) \(X_1|X_2=x_2\) is not normal

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(\mathrm{Cov}(X_1+X_2,X_1-X_2)=\sigma_1^2-\sigma_2^2\). This is zero iff \(\sigma_1=\sigma_2\); under bivariate normality, zero covariance implies independence. (2) For bivariate normal, uncorrelated (\(\rho=0\)) implies independent. (3) False: for bivariate normal, \(\rho=0\) implies independence. (4) False: conditional distribution \(X_1|X_2=x_2\) is normal.

name.text: NOV2020 Q89 - OLS estimator in regression through origin

questiontext.text:
Let \((X_1,Y_1),\ldots,(X_n,Y_n)\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1^2]<\infty\) and \(E[Y_1^2]<\infty\). Let \(\hat{\beta}\) be the OLS estimator of \(\beta\) in \(Y=\beta X\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\hat{\beta}=\dfrac{\sum X_iY_i}{\sum X_i^2}\)
(2) \(\hat{\beta}\xrightarrow{P}\dfrac{E[XY]}{E[X^2]}\)
(3) \(\hat{\beta}\) is always unbiased
(4) \(\hat{\beta}\) is the MLE under all distributions

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) OLS minimises \(\sum(Y_i-\beta X_i)^2\): derivative gives \(\hat{\beta}=\sum X_iY_i/\sum X_i^2\). (2) By LLN, \(\hat{\beta}\to E[XY]/E[X^2]\) in probability. (3) Unbiased only when \(E[Y|X]=\beta X\); not always. (4) MLE depends on the error distribution assumption.

answer.text: \(\hat{\beta}\) is the MLE under all distributions

name.text: NOV2020 Q90 - MLE, completeness, and UMVUE for Bernoulli

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. \(\mathrm{Bernoulli}(\theta)\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bar{X}\) is the MLE of \(\theta\)
(2) \(\bar{X}\) is a complete sufficient statistic
(3) \(\bar{X}\) is the UMVUE of \(\theta\)
(4) \(\bar{X}^2\) is the UMVUE of \(\theta^2\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Likelihood is maximised at \(\hat{\theta}=\bar{X}\). (2) \(T=\sum X_i\) (equivalently \(\bar{X}\)) is complete sufficient for the Bernoulli exponential family. (3) Since \(\bar{X}\) is an unbiased function of the complete sufficient statistic, it is the UMVUE. (4) \(\bar{X}^2\) is biased for \(\theta^2\); the UMVUE of \(\theta^2\) is \(\frac{n\bar{X}^2-\bar{X}}{n-1}\) (using Rao-Blackwell).
